summary: Gruber responds to Steve Jobs's January 2009 announcement that he is taking a medical leave of absence from Apple until the end of June, just one week after Jobs had downplayed his health issues as a treatable 'hormone imbalance.' Gruber notes the sudden shift in tone and the cryptic language about health issues being 'more complex than originally thought,' reading it as clearly meaning 'worse.' He contrasts the six-month leave with Jobs's much quicker recovery from pancreatic cancer surgery in 2004, suggesting these issues are obviously quite serious. He explicitly declines to demand more private health details, closing with a brief, emotional expression of goodwill.
key_points: Jobs's leave announcement was a jarring reversal from his reassuring statement just one week earlier about a simple hormone imbalance
The euphemism 'more complex' almost certainly means 'worse,' and the six-month timeline suggests something far more serious than previously disclosed
Gruber pushes back against press demands for more health details, respecting Jobs's right to privacy
Compared to Jobs's rapid return after 2004 cancer surgery, a six-month leave is an ominous signal about severity
money_quotes: Unlike many others in the technical (and financial) press, I "demand" to know nothing further.
If this is all Jobs chooses to share regarding the specific details of what ails him, that is up to him.
But it's very difficult to read this any other way than that where Jobs says "more complex", he means "worse".
Six months is a long time, especially for someone like Jobs who clearly loves his job.
There's not a word in today's announcement that can be interpreted as good news.
key_insight: Jobs's sudden shift from reassurance to a six-month medical leave, couched in careful euphemism, left no room for optimistic interpretation despite the press's demand for more details.
